content:
# Leviticus Chapter 3 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 58v–59r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_003_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_003_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 87–88 (pa_0091–pa_0092)

---

**1** And if his offering is a sacrifice of peace offerings — if he offers from the herd, whether male or female, he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD.

**2** And he shall lay his hand on the head of his offering and slaughter it at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall throw the blood against the sides of the altar.

**3** And from the sacrifice of peace offerings he shall bring a fire offering to the LORD: the fat covering the entrails, and all the fat that is on the entrails,

**4** and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys.

**5** And the sons of Aaron shall burn it on the altar on the burnt offering, which is on the wood on the fire, a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**6** And if his offering for a sacrifice of peace offerings to the LORD is from the flock, whether male or female, he shall offer it without blemish.

**7** If he offers a lamb for his offering, he shall bring it before the LORD,

**8** and he shall lay his hand on the head of his offering and slaughter it before the tent of meeting, and the sons of Aaron shall throw its blood against the sides of the altar.

**9** And from the sacrifice of peace offerings he shall bring a fire offering to the LORD: its fat, the whole fat tail removed close to the backbone, the fat covering the entrails, and all the fat that is on the entrails,

**10** and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys.

**11** And the priest shall burn it on the altar as food, a fire offering to the LORD.

**12** And if his offering is a goat, he shall bring it before the LORD,

**13** and he shall lay his hand on its head and slaughter it before the tent of meeting, and the sons of Aaron shall throw its blood against the sides of the altar.

**14** And he shall bring from it his offering, a fire offering to the LORD: the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails,

**15** and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys.

**16** And the priest shall burn them on the altar as food, a fire offering, a pleasing aroma. All fat belongs to the LORD.

**17** It is a perpetual statute throughout your generations, in all your dwelling places: you shall eat neither fat nor blood.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 58v–59r.** Both pages of the Leningrad Codex were examined directly. Page one of the manuscript image (folio 58v) shows the three-column format of the B19A in clear condition, with the Masorah parva notations in the outer margin and between columns. The chapter opens mid-folio, following immediately from the grain offering legislation of chapter 2 without a major section break. The repeated structural formula וְאִם-זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים (ve-im zevach shelamim, "and if a sacrifice of peace offerings") is visible marking the three-animal subsections (herd: v. 1; flock-lamb: v. 6; goat: v. 12). The careful repetition of the fat-and-kidney anatomy formula (vv. 3–4, 9–10, 14–15) is a prominent visual feature of the text in both columns.

Folio 59r (page two) carries the continuation through verse 17, concluding the chapter with the sweeping prohibition of v. 17: the chuqqat olam formula followed by the double prohibition on fat and blood. The Masorah parva mark on v. 17 is consistent with the chapter's status as a major legislative close. The notation on chelev throughout is consonantally clear; the dagesh and vowel points distinguishing chelev (suet/fat) from shumen (oil) are fully preserved.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 87–88 (LXX).** Page 87 (pa_0091) was examined. It bears the header "CAP. XXXI" at its upper right (corresponding to the LXX's chapter numbering for this section of Leviticus), and the text continues from the end of chapter 2 material visible at the top of the page. The Greek uncial text is written in three columns with red-ink chapter notation in the margin. The LXX text for Leviticus 3 follows the MT with a number of characteristic lexical choices noted below. Page 88 (pa_0092) carries the continuation of the chapter and its conclusion. The paragraph break before v. 17's final prohibition is visible in the manuscript as a slight spacing in the text column.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **Zevach Shelamim / "sacrifice of peace offerings" (vv. 1, 3, 6, 9).** Hebrew זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים (zevach shelamim) is the single most theologically dense term of this chapter. It is a compound: zevach ("sacrifice," from the root zabah, "to slaughter") + shelamim (a plural intensive noun from shalom, "peace, wholeness, well-being"). The term designates the only offering in the Levitical system where the worshipper keeps and eats a portion — shared between the offerer, the priest, and God (via the altar smoke). It is simultaneously a meal, a peace-covenant ceremony, and a communion act. The plural shelamim has been the subject of extensive scholarly debate: it may denote a plural of extension (intensive singular), plural of result ("that which produces peace"), or a category plural (the genre of well-being offerings as a class).

   This translation uses "sacrifice of peace offerings" — the standard formal equivalence rendering (ESV, NASB, KJV) — which preserves both components of the compound. "Fellowship offering" (NIV) captures the communal dimension well but loses the shalom root. "Well-being offering" (NJPS) is more lexically precise regarding the semantic field of shalom but departs from established liturgical English. "Peace offering" is retained, albeit qualified by "sacrifice of" to preserve the zevach element. The LXX renders εἰρηνική ("peace offering / peaceable sacrifice"), a direct rendering of shelamim from εἰρήνη (eirene, "peace"), confirming the semantic derivation.

2. **Chelev / "fat" (vv. 3, 4, 9, 10, 14, 15, 16, 17).** Hebrew חֵלֶב (chelev) is a precise technical term for the suet — the hard internal fat surrounding the kidneys, the stomach cavity, and the loin region. It is distinct from the general marbling fat within the meat (which was not prohibited) and from the fatty tail of the fat-tail sheep (alya, v. 9). Chelev belongs exclusively to the LORD; it is not to be eaten (v. 17). This translation renders chelev as "fat" throughout, as English "fat" must be understood in context as the specified internal suet, not intramuscular fat. The LXX renders στέαρ (stear, "suet, hard fat"), which is a more precise Greek term than the general λίπος ("fat"), confirming the technical character of chelev.

3. **"The fat covering the entrails, and all the fat on the entrails" (vv. 3, 9, 14).** The repeated formula — הַחֵלֶב הַמְכַסֶּה אֶת-הַקֶּרֶב וְאֵת כָּל-הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עַל-הַקֶּרֶב — describes two components: (a) the omentum (the covering fat apron over the stomach, "the fat covering the entrails") and (b) the mesentery and other fatty deposits on the internal organs ("all the fat that is on the entrails"). The translation renders this anatomically, preserving the twofold structure. This is the same formula in all three animal subsections, and its verbatim repetition is maintained.

4. **"The lobe of the liver" (vv. 4, 10, 15).** Hebrew יֹתֶרֶת עַל-הַכָּבֵד (yoteret al ha-kaved) — literally "the appendage that is over the liver." The yoteret is most plausibly identified with the caudate lobe of the liver (sometimes called the "lobe" or "appendage" in anatomical literature). "Lobe of the liver" follows formal equivalence conventions (ESV, NASB). The LXX renders λοβὸν τοῦ ἥπατος ("lobe of the liver"), in direct agreement. The phrase "which he shall remove with the kidneys" (יְסִירֶנָּה עִם הַכְּלָיֹת) indicates the yoteret was extracted together with the kidneys as a unit; the translation renders this clarifying clause faithfully.

5. **"The whole fat tail removed close to the backbone" (v. 9).** The fat-tail sheep (Ovis orientalis, the broad-tailed or fat-tailed breed common in the ancient Near East) is specifically addressed in the lamb subsection only. Hebrew הָאַלְיָה תְּמִימָה לְעֻמַּת הֶעָצֶה יְסִירֶנָּה (ha-alya temimah le-ummat he-atseh yesurenah) — "the whole fat tail, opposite/against the backbone, he shall remove it." Alya designates the entire fatty tail-mass of the broad-tailed sheep, a substantial organ weighing several kilograms in mature animals. "Removed close to the backbone" renders le-ummat he-atseh ("opposite the vertebra / at the spine") — indicating the surgical cut is made at the tail's attachment to the spine. This anatomical precision is preserved in the translation. The LXX renders τὸν λοβὸν τῆς ὀσφύος ἄμωμον ("the lobe of the loin, blameless/whole"), which condenses and slightly re-interprets; the MT's alya (fat tail) is more specific.

6. **"Food" / Lechem (vv. 11, 16).** Hebrew לֶחֶם (lechem) normally means "bread" or "food." In the cultic context of vv. 11 and 16 — "the priest shall burn it on the altar as food, a fire offering to the LORD" — lechem is used in its broader sense of "food" to describe what is being offered to God. The phrase reflects the ancient Near Eastern conceptual framework in which sacrifices were understood as "food" placed before the deity. This translation renders it "food" rather than "bread" to reflect the contextual semantic range. The LXX renders κάρπωμα ("burnt offering / gift offering"), which avoids the food-metaphor and substitutes a general sacrificial term, a significant interpretive divergence noted in the variant table below.

7. **Dam / "blood" (vv. 2, 8, 13; prohibition: v. 17).** Hebrew דָּם (dam, "blood") is rendered "blood" throughout. The blood ritual — throwing or dashing the blood against the sides of the altar — is the primary priestly action that constitutes the valid offering. The blood belongs to the LORD by reason of the life that is in it (Lev 17:11, 14). This translation distinguishes clearly between the blood-application ritual (vv. 2, 8, 13) and the blood-prohibition of v. 17, presenting the two uses of dam in their full structural relationship. The LXX renders αἷμα ("blood") throughout, in agreement.

8. **Chuqqat Olam / "perpetual statute" (v. 17).** Hebrew חֻקַּת עוֹלָם (chuqqat olam) is the strongest legislative formula in the Pentateuch, combining chuqqah ("statute, prescribed law, inviolable ordinance") with olam ("perpetuity, forever, across all time"). The phrase frames v. 17's twin prohibition — no fat, no blood — as an eternal, universal obligation incumbent on Israel across all generations and all dwelling places. The triple emphasis of the verse: chuqqat olam, "throughout your generations" (le-doroteikhem), "in all your dwelling places" (be-khol moshevoteikhem) — stresses the unbounded geographical and temporal reach of the law. "Perpetual statute" is the standard formal rendering (ESV, NASB); "lasting ordinance" (NIV) is a legitimate alternative but loses the temporal absoluteness of olam. The LXX renders νόμιμον αἰώνιον ("everlasting ordinance / eternal law"), in close agreement, using αἰώνιον (aionion) as the Greek equivalent of olam.

9. **"Before the LORD" vs. "before the tent of meeting" (vv. 1, 7, 12).** A subtle variation exists between the three animal subsections. In the herd subsection (v. 1), the offering is presented "before the LORD" (לִפְנֵי יְהוָה), and the slaughter occurs "at the entrance of the tent of meeting" (v. 2). In the lamb and goat subsections (vv. 7–8; 12–13), the animal is brought "before the LORD" and slaughtered "before the tent of meeting" (לִפְנֵי אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד). The translation preserves these distinctions as they appear in the MT, as they reflect deliberate compositional shaping. The LXX normalizes somewhat by using ἔναντι Κυρίου ("before the LORD") and ἐπὶ τὰς θύρας τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("at the doors of the tent of witness") consistently.

10. **"All fat belongs to the LORD" (v. 16b).** The clause כָּל-חֵלֶב לַיהוָה ("all fat belongs to the LORD") stands as a categorical declaration at the close of the third subsection, functioning as a summary statement before the final prohibition. It is the theological climax of all three fat-offering lists. This translation places it as a full independent sentence to honor its declaratory weight, rather than subordinating it as a subordinate clause. The LXX renders πᾶν τὸ στέαρ τῷ Κυρίῳ ("all the suet to the LORD"), in close agreement.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 87–88) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3:1 | zevach shelamim ("sacrifice of peace offerings") | θυσία σωτηρίου ("sacrifice of salvation / deliverance") | LXX renders shelamim as σωτήριον (sotерion, "salvation, deliverance") rather than εἰρηνική ("peaceable"). This is a significant semantic shift: LXX interprets the shalam root through the lens of "salvation/deliverance" rather than "peace/wholeness." This translation follows the MT, rendering "peace offerings." |
| 3:5 | "on the burnt offering" (al ha-olah) | ἐπὶ τῶν ξύλων ("upon the wood") | MT specifies that the peace offering fat is laid "on the burnt offering" — i.e., on top of the olah already burning on the altar. LXX reads "upon the wood," omitting the reference to the burnt offering. The MT's reading is more liturgically specific and probably original; LXX may reflect a simplification. This translation follows the MT. |
| 3:9 | alya temimah ("the whole fat tail") | λοβὸν τῆς ὀσφύος ἄμωμον ("the lobe of the loin, without blemish") | LXX does not use a term for the fat tail (alya), instead rendering "lobe of the loin." This may reflect a different anatomical understanding, or the LXX translator's unfamiliarity with the specifically Levantine fat-tailed sheep breed. MT's alya is the more precise and culturally specific term. |
| 3:11 | lechem ("food") | κάρπωμα ("burnt offering / gift to be burned") | MT uses lechem (literally "bread / food") to describe the peace offering fat as "food" for the LORD — an anthropomorphic metaphor. LXX avoids this language and substitutes κάρπωμα (a general term for a burnt sacrifice), softening the anthropomorphism. This translation follows MT's "food." |
| 3:16 | lechem ("food") | κάρπωμα ("burnt offering") | Same pattern as v. 11. LXX substitutes κάρπωμα for MT's lechem in both peace-offering subsections (lamb and goat), consistently avoiding the "food" metaphor. |
| 3:16 | "All fat belongs to the LORD" (kol chelev la-YHWH) | πᾶν τὸ στέαρ τῷ Κυρίῳ | LXX renders στέαρ (stear, "suet") rather than a general fat term, confirming that chelev is understood as hard internal suet, not general body fat. Close agreement with MT. |
| 3:17 | chuqqat olam ("perpetual statute") | νόμιμον αἰώνιον ("everlasting ordinance") | LXX uses νόμιμον (nomikon, "that which pertains to law / ordinance") for chuqqah, and αἰώνιον for olam. Close semantic agreement; both stress the eternal, legislated character of the prohibition. |
| 3:17 | "in all your dwelling places" (be-khol moshevoteikhem) | ἐν πάσαις ταῖς κατοικίαις ὑμῶν ("in all your dwelling places") | Direct agreement; LXX renders κατοικία ("dwelling, residence") for moshev. |
| 3:17 | dual prohibition: "neither fat nor blood" | οὔτε στέαρ οὔτε αἷμα ("neither suet nor blood") | LXX again uses στέαρ (suet) for chelev, consistent with v. 16. Full agreement on the dual prohibition. |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 3)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים (zevach shelamim) | sacrifice of peace offerings | Communal well-being offering; offerer retains and eats a portion |
| חֵלֶב (chelev) | fat | Specifically internal suet; belongs exclusively to the LORD |
| אַלְיָה (alya) | fat tail | The large fatty tail of the broad-tailed sheep; given to the LORD in full |
| יֹתֶרֶת (yoteret) | lobe [of the liver] | Caudate lobe of the liver; removed with the kidneys as a unit |
| לֶחֶם (lechem, cultic) | food | Anthropomorphic term for the sacrificial portion as "food" for the LORD |
| חֻקַּת עוֹלָם (chuqqat olam) | perpetual statute | Strongest legislative formula; unbounded temporal and geographic scope |
| דָּם (dam) | blood | Thrown against altar sides in the ritual; prohibited for eating (v. 17) |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Established from Lev 1; the whole-ascending offering |
| מִנְחָה (minchah) | grain offering | Established from Lev 2; the cereal tribute-offering |
| אִשֶּׁה (isheh) | fire offering | Established from Lev 1; the category of offerings by fire |
| רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach) | pleasing aroma | Anthropomorphic idiom; consistent throughout |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד | tent of meeting | Established convention |
| קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qodashim) | most holy portion | Established from Lev 2 |

— LOGOS
